title: Roll and Play Fiction and Fantasy - Solo RPG Tools
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fFdiFTfeCwE

A holiday-season utility review: Daniel (@dungeondive) looks at two books of random tables from Roll & Play Press — the Sci-Fi Toolkit and the Fantasy Toolkit, both by Sam Bartlett and Beth Davies — and comes away with a clear favourite. The sci-fi volume delights him: 120+ tables covering missions, alien rulers, ship malfunctions, warp side effects, black-market goods, glitching-hologram barkeeps, and even a planet's pop culture — its music, art, and comics. He plans to keep it shelved right next to Space Aces, and marvels that the publisher never markets these as solo tools, missing an obvious audience of oracle-hungry soloists. The fantasy volume is where the review gets pointed. It's useful — he praises the town generators, supernatural patrons (perfect for Lankhmar-style play), and bargain spell scrolls that might turn out to be Fire Bowl instead of Firebolt — but the anachronisms grate: taverns playing ska, reggae, and smooth funk, and a magic item dispensing infinite tomato ketchup. In a wacky future setting that extrapolation works, he argues; in a serious fantasy world it breaks the spell, and he'd reach for Madeline Hale's Table Fables first. Verdict: buy the sci-fi one first, but both will see table time.

----

Daniel forgives ska bands in a sci-fi cantina but not in a fantasy tavern. Where's your line on anachronism in games — flavour or immersion-breaker?
